subject: Physics
q:
The displacement x versus time graph is shown below.

(A) The average velocity during 0 to 3 s is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(B) The average velocity during 3 to 5 s is $0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(C) The instantaneous velocity at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(D) The average velocity during 5 to 7 s and instantaneous velocity at $\mathrm{t}=6.5 \mathrm{~s}$ are equal
(E) The average velocity from $t=0$ to $t=9 \mathrm{~s}$ is zero
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The displacement x versus time graph is shown below.

(A) The average velocity during 0 to 3 s is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(B) The average velocity during 3 to 5 s is $0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(C) The instantaneous velocity at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(D) The average velocity during 5 to 7 s and instantaneous velocity at $\mathrm{t}=6.5 \mathrm{~s}$ are equal
(E) The average velocity from $t=0$ to $t=9 \mathrm{~s}$ is zero
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \langle\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}\rangle=\frac{\Delta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{s}}}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{i}}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\text { slope }
\end{aligned}$
(A) 0 to $3 \mathrm{sec} ;\langle\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}\rangle=\frac{5-0}{3}=5 / 3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(B) 0 to $5 \mathrm{sec} ;\langle\vec{v}\rangle=\frac{5-5}{2}=0$
(C) $\mathrm{t}=2 ;$ slope $=\vec{v}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
(D) $\mathfrak{t}=5$ to $7 \mathrm{sec} ;\langle\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}\rangle=\frac{0-5}{2}=-2.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
At $t=6.5 \mathrm{sec} ; \vec{v}=10$
(E) $\mathrm{t}=0$ to $\mathrm{t}=9 ;\langle\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}\rangle=0$

subject: Physics
q: A balloon was moving upwards with a uniform velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. An object of finite mass is dropped from the balloon when it was at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the ground level. The height of the balloon from the ground when object strikes the ground was around: (takes the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A balloon was moving upwards with a uniform velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. An object of finite mass is dropped from the balloon when it was at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the ground level. The height of the balloon from the ground when object strikes the ground was around: (takes the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Object is projected as shown so as per motion under gravity, 

S=ut+12at2

-75=+10t+12(-10)t2⇒t=5 s

Object takes t=5 s to fall on ground height of balloon from ground, 

H=75+ut

=75+10×5=125 m

subject: Physics
q: Position of an ant ( S in metres) moving in Y-Z plane is given by S=2t2j^+5k^ (where t is in second). The magnitude and direction of velocity of the ant at t=1 s will be :
question: Position of an ant ( S in metres) moving in Y-Z plane is given by S=2t2j^+5k^ (where t is in second). The magnitude and direction of velocity of the ant at t=1 s will be :
solution:
The velocity of the ant can be found as follows:

v→=dS→dt=ddt2t2j^+5k^=4tj^   ...1

At t=1 s, from equation (1), it follows that

v→=4j^

subject: Physics
q: If the velocity of a body related to displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>5000</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>24</mn><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the acceleration of the body is _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
question: If the velocity of a body related to displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>5000</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>24</mn><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the acceleration of the body is _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
We know that the rate of change of linear velocity is called linear acceleration, a=dvdt=dv×dxdt×dx=vdvdx

⇒a=5000+24x×125000+24x×24=12 m s-2.

q: Starting from the origin at time <math><mrow><mtext>t</mtext><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></math>, with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>⏜</mo></mover><mo> </mo><msup><mtext>ms</mtext><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, a particle moves in the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -<math><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>⏜</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>⏜</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mtext>ms</mtext><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At time <math><mtext>t</mtext></math>, its coordinates are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mtext>m, y</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mtext> m</mtext></mrow></mfenced></math>. The values of <math><mtext>t</mtext></math> and <math><mrow><msub><mtext>y</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></math> are, respectively:
question: Starting from the origin at time <math><mrow><mtext>t</mtext><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></math>, with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>⏜</mo></mover><mo> </mo><msup><mtext>ms</mtext><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, a particle moves in the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -<math><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>⏜</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>⏜</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mtext>ms</mtext><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At time <math><mtext>t</mtext></math>, its coordinates are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mtext>m, y</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mtext> m</mtext></mrow></mfenced></math>. The values of <math><mtext>t</mtext></math> and <math><mrow><msub><mtext>y</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></math> are, respectively:
solution:
Equation 1

Sx=12axt2

20=12×10×t2

t=2

Equation 2

Sy=uyt+12ayt2

y=52+12422

y=18

subject: Physics
q: A particle starts from origin at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and moves in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> plane under action of a force which produces a constant acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-coordinate of the particle at that instant is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, then the speed of the particle at this time is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _______.
question: A particle starts from origin at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and moves in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> plane under action of a force which produces a constant acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-coordinate of the particle at that instant is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, then the speed of the particle at this time is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
Given, ux=5 m s-1, ax=3 m s-2, x=84 m

The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle along x-axis is given by

vx2=ux2+2ax   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

vx2=25+2(3)(84)⇒vx=23 m s-1

Also, the velocity of the particle along x-axis can be written as

vx=ux+axt   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that

t=23-53=6 s

Similarly, for the y- component of the velocity, it follows that

vy=0+ayt=0+2×(6)=12 m s-1

Hence, the magnitude of the velocity is given by

v2=vx2+vy2=232+122=673v=673 m s-1

Therefore, α=673.

subject: Physics
q: A person travelling on a straight line moves with a uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}_1$ for a distance x and with a uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}_2$ for the next $\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{x}$ distance. The average velocity in this motion is $\frac{50}{7} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If $\mathrm{v}_1$ is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ then $\mathrm{v}_2=$ _________ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: A person travelling on a straight line moves with a uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}_1$ for a distance x and with a uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}_2$ for the next $\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{x}$ distance. The average velocity in this motion is $\frac{50}{7} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If $\mathrm{v}_1$ is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ then $\mathrm{v}_2=$ _________ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}_{\text {avg }}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{x}_2}{\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{50}{7}=\frac{\mathrm{x}+\frac{3 \mathrm{x}}{2}}{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{5}+\frac{3 \mathrm{x}}{2 \mathrm{v}_2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{50}{7}=\frac{5 / 2}{\frac{1}{5}+\frac{3}{2 \mathrm{v}_2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{5}+\frac{3}{2 \mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{7}{20}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2 \mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{7}{20}-\frac{1}{5}=\frac{7-4}{20} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2 \mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{3}{20} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_2=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A person climbs up a stalled escalator in $60 \mathrm{~s}$. If standing on the same but escalator running with constant velocity he takes $40 \mathrm{~s}$. How much time is taken by the person to walk up the moving escalator?
question: A person climbs up a stalled escalator in $60 \mathrm{~s}$. If standing on the same but escalator running with constant velocity he takes $40 \mathrm{~s}$. How much time is taken by the person to walk up the moving escalator?
solution:
Person's speed walking only is $\frac{1}{60} \frac{\text { "escalator" }}{\text { second }}$
Standing the escalator without walking the
speed is $\frac{1}{40} \frac{\text { "escalator" }}{\text { second }}$
Walking with the escalator going, the speed add.
So, the person's speed is $\frac{1}{60}+\frac{1}{40}=\frac{15}{120}$
$\frac{\text { "escalator" }}{\text { second }}$
So, the time to go up the escalator ("seconds per escalator") is

$$
t=\frac{120}{5}=24 \text { second }
$$

subject: Physics
q: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the velocity of the train B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then length of train B is:
question: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the velocity of the train B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then length of train B is:
solution:
Let the velocity of A be vA and the velocity of B be vB.

The relative velocity is

v→BA =v→B-v→A⇒vBA= 54-(-90)=144 km h-1

⇒vBA= 40 m s-1

Time=lengthvBA 

⇒length= 40 m s-1 × 8 s=320 m

subject: Physics
q: Two buses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> start from a point at the same time and move in a straight line and their positions are represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>Q</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. At what time, both the buses have same velocity ?
question: Two buses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> start from a point at the same time and move in a straight line and their positions are represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>Q</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. At what time, both the buses have same velocity ?
solution:
As we know, v=dxdt.

Given, xPt=αt+βt2

Velocity of bus P is vP=dxPdt=α+2βt and velocity of bus Q is  vQ=f-2t

When vP=vQ

⇒α+2βt=f-2t

 ⇒f-α=2β+1t ⇒t=f-α2β+1

subject: Physics
q: A person standing on a spring balance inside a stationary lift measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The weight of that person if the lift descends with uniform downward acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be <u>              </u> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A person standing on a spring balance inside a stationary lift measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The weight of that person if the lift descends with uniform downward acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be <u>              </u> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
When lift is at rest

N=mg

⇒60×10=600 N

When lift moves with downward acceleration. In frame of lift pseudo force will be in upward direction.

N'=Mg-a

⇒6010-1.8

N'⇒492 N

question: The speed verses time graph for a particle is shown in the figure. The distance travelled (in m) by the particle during the time interval t=0 to t=5 s will be __________
solution:
Distance = Area of |v| – t graph 

=1/2×8×5=20 m

q: A particle is moving unidirectional on a horizontal plane under the action of a constant power supplying energy source. The displacement (s) – time (t) graph that describes the motion of the particle is (graphs are drawn schematically and are not to scale):
question: A particle is moving unidirectional on a horizontal plane under the action of a constant power supplying energy source. The displacement (s) – time (t) graph that describes the motion of the particle is (graphs are drawn schematically and are not to scale):
solution:
Here Power is constant,
Hence, P=Fv=mav=av=constant.

av=kvdvdx×v=k∫0vv2dv=k∫0xdx∴v=3k1/3×x1/3⇒v=Ax1/3 where A=3k1/3=constant ....1

Also, v=dxdt∴dxdt=Ax1/3⇒∫0ddxx1/3=A∫0tdt

Simplifying further we get,

⇒d=At3/2    ...2

From equations 1 & 2 its clear that the graph won't be straight line so first & third options cant be correct.
Now, from equation 1 its clear that when x is increased then the slope dsdt should also increase which is possible in second option. Hence option 2 is correct.

q: An elevator in a building can carry a maximum of <math><mn>10</mn></math> persons, with the average mass of each person being <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>68</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> . The mass of the elevator itself is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>920</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and it moves with a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> . The frictional force opposing the motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> . If the elevator is moving up with its full capacity, the power delivered by the motor to the elevator <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></math> must be at least:
question: An elevator in a building can carry a maximum of <math><mn>10</mn></math> persons, with the average mass of each person being <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>68</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> . The mass of the elevator itself is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>920</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and it moves with a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> . The frictional force opposing the motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> . If the elevator is moving up with its full capacity, the power delivered by the motor to the elevator <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></math> must be at least:
solution:
Net force on motor will be
Fm=920+6810g+6000
=22000 N
So, required power for motor
Pm=F→m.v→
=22000×3
=66000 watt

question:

A
B

solution:
A. Acceleration =  constant; a v→t graph will be constant line parallel to time axis. Option A is correct.
B. v=u+at ; velocity vs time graph will be straight line passing through origin. Option B is correct.
u=0;  initially at rest
v= at
C. X=12at2 (particle was initially at origin) v→t graph will be parabola passing through origin. Option D is correct.

a→=F→m=2i^+3j^+5k^2

=i^+1.5j^+2.5k^

τ→=u→t+12a→t2

=0+12i^+1.5j^+2.5k^16

=8i^+12j^+20k^

b=12

q: A tennis ball is dropped on to the floor from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. It rebounds to a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Ball comes in contact with the floor for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The average acceleration during contact is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A tennis ball is dropped on to the floor from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. It rebounds to a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Ball comes in contact with the floor for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The average acceleration during contact is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
The speed of ball just before collision with ground is vi2=0+2ghi

 ⇒vi=2ghi

=2×10×9.8

=14 m s-1  Downward

The speed of ball just after collision is 0=vf2-2ghf

⇒vf=2ghf

=2×10×5

=10 m s-1  Upward

Average acceleration of ball is a→avg=Δv→Δt=vf--vit=10+140.2=240.2=120 m s-2.

subject: Physics
q: An object is allowed to fall from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> above the earth, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of earth. Its velocity when it strikes the earth’s surface, ignoring air resistance, will be :
question: An object is allowed to fall from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> above the earth, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of earth. Its velocity when it strikes the earth’s surface, ignoring air resistance, will be :
solution:
As gravitational field is a conservative field and therefore mechanical energy will be conserved. Therefore,
Loss in PE = Gain in KE

-GMm2R--GMmR=12mv2

⇒v2=GMR=gRas g=GMR2

⇒v=gR

Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is released from the top at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is thrown vertically down with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. After a certain time, both balls meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground. Find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: Two balls <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are placed at the top of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall tower. Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is released from the top at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is thrown vertically down with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. After a certain time, both balls meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground. Find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Displacement covered by first ball,180-100=0+12×10×t2   ⇒t=4 s

Now, the second body gets only,  t2=4-2=2 s.

Displacement covered by second will be same,

80=u×2+12×10×22

⇒u=80-202=30 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A goods train accelerating uniformly on a straight railway track, approaches an electric pole standing on the side of track. Its engine passes the pole with velocity $u$ and the guard's room passes with velocity $v$. The middle wagon of the train passes the pole with a velocity.
question: A goods train accelerating uniformly on a straight railway track, approaches an electric pole standing on the side of track. Its engine passes the pole with velocity $u$ and the guard's room passes with velocity $v$. The middle wagon of the train passes the pole with a velocity.
solution:
Let ' $S$ ' be the distance between two ends ' $a$ ' be the constant acceleration As we know $V^2-u^2=2 a S$
or, $a S=\frac{V^2-u^2}{2}$
Let $V$ be velocity at mid point.
Therefore, $V_c^2-u^2=2 a \frac{S}{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& V_c^2=u^2+a S \\
& V_c^2=u^2+\frac{V^2-u^2}{2} \\
& V_c=\sqrt{\frac{u^2+v^2}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: An automobile, travelling at $40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can be stopped at a distance of $40 \mathrm{~m}$ by applying brakes. If the same automobile is travelling at $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the minimum stopping distance, in metres, is (assume no skidding)
question: An automobile, travelling at $40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can be stopped at a distance of $40 \mathrm{~m}$ by applying brakes. If the same automobile is travelling at $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the minimum stopping distance, in metres, is (assume no skidding)
solution:
According to question, $u_1=40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}, v_1=0$ and $s_1=40 \mathrm{~m}$ using $v^2-u^2=2 a s ; 0^2-40^2=2 a \times 40$..(i) Again, $0^2-80^2=2$ as From eqn. (i) and (ii)
Stopping distance, $s=160 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown vertically up (taken as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from the ground. The correct momentum-height <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>)</mo></math> diagram is:
question: A ball is thrown vertically up (taken as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from the ground. The correct momentum-height <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>)</mo></math> diagram is:
solution:
If the ball was thrown with initial velocity u , then its velocity at height 'h' can be written as.
v=u2-2gh
Its linear momentum, p=mv=mu2-2gh
Or  p2=m2u2-2m2gh
The graph between p & h will be a parabola.
In motion when particle was going up, its velocity is positive and decreasing with height and so is the linear momentum. When it reaches the maximum height, the velocity and linear momentum will be zero. Again, it will start to fall back, the velocity and linear momentum will increase but will have negative value.

q: The distance travelled by a particle is related to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The velocity of the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is
question: The distance travelled by a particle is related to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. solution:
Given here, x = 4t2

We know that velocity is the rate at which displacement changes with time. So, v=dxdt=d4t2dt=8t

Now, at t=5 s

Velocity of particle is v=8×5=40 m s-1.

q: Consider a rubber ball freely falling from a height $h=4.9 \mathrm{~m}$ onto a horizontal elastic plate. Assume that the duration of collision is negligible and the collision with the plate is totally elastic. Then the velocity as a function of time the height as function of time will be
question: Consider a rubber ball freely falling from a height $h=4.9 \mathrm{~m}$ onto a horizontal elastic plate. Assume that the duration of collision is negligible and the collision with the plate is totally elastic. Then the velocity as a function of time the height as function of time will be
solution: $\mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^2, \quad \mathrm{v}=-\mathrm{gt}$ and after the collision, $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{gt}$. (parabolic) (straight line) Collision is perfectly elastic then ball reaches to same height again and again with same velocity.

q: Which graph corresponds to an object moving with a constant negative acceleration and a positive velocity?
question: Which graph corresponds to an object moving with a constant negative acceleration and a positive velocity?
solution:
Let acceleration be a.
Given that a=-C
We know that, a=vdvdx=-C
vdv=-Cdx

Integrating the above equation,
v22=-Cx+k.

Thus, the velocity-distance graph is

The distance cover by the object is x= -v22C+kC.

Thus, with the increase in distance, velocity decreases.

Now, a=-C=dvdt

⇒dv=-Cdt⇒v=-Ct+u , straight line

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves along the $x$-axis and has its displacement $x$ varying with time $t$ according to the equation $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{c}_0\left(\mathrm{t}^2-2\right)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t}-2)^2$ where $c_0$ and $c$ are constants of appropriate dimensions. Then, which of the following statements is correct?
question: A particle moves along the $x$-axis and has its displacement $x$ varying with time $t$ according to the equation $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{c}_0\left(\mathrm{t}^2-2\right)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t}-2)^2$ where $c_0$ and $c$ are constants of appropriate dimensions. Then, which of the following statements is correct?
options: the acceleration of the particle is $2 \mathrm{c}_0$
the acceleration of the particle is $2\mathrm{~c}$
the initial velocity of the particle is $4\mathrm{~c}$
the acceleration of the particle is $2\left(c+c_0\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{tC}_0+2 \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{t}-2) \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{C}_0+2 \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Area under velocity-time graph gives the distance travelled by the body in a given time.
Statement II: Area under acceleration-time graph is equal to the change in velocity in the given time.
In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Area under velocity-time graph gives the distance travelled by the body in a given time.
Statement II: Area under acceleration-time graph is equal to the change in velocity in the given time.
In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
solution:
The definition of displacement is given by the product of velocity and time.

∫vdt=∆x=displacement of body in given time.

Distance is a scalar quantity and displacement is a vector quantity defined by the shortest distance between the initial and the final position.

Acceleration is defined by the rate of change of velocity.
∫adt=∆v=change in velocity in given time.

Thus, the area under the velocity time graph gives the displacement of the body and the area under the acceleration time graph gives the change in velocity of the body.

subject: Physics
q: Form the v - t graph shown, the ratio of distance to displacement in 25 s of motion is:
question: Form the v - t graph shown, the ratio of distance to displacement in 25 s of motion is:
solution:
The area under the velocity time graph gives the displacement. The area for t=0 s to t=25 s is 

(Area)=12×5×10+5×10+12×30×5+12×20×5-12×20×5

 ⇒Area=25+50+75+50-50=150 m
Hence, the displacement =150 m

The distance is defined as the path taken by the object to travel from initial to final position. Also note that displacement is a vector quantity, but distance is a scalar quantity.
Distance =12×5×10+5×10+12×30×5+12×20×5+12×20×5=(200+50)=250 m

Therefore, required ratio
 distance  displacement =250150=53
